title: Classes & Objects
subtitle: Build your own LEGO-style blueprints, then snap real things out of them.
goal: Understand what a class and an object are, write a class with fields, a constructor, and instance methods, and make several objects from it using the new keyword.

blocks.heading: Blueprints and the things you build 🏗️
blocks.html:
Imagine a cookie cutter. The cutter itself is not a cookie — it is the shape that decides how every cookie will look. Press it into the dough once, twice, ten times, and you get ten cookies. They share the same shape, but each one is its own cookie.

In Java a class is the cookie cutter (the blueprint). An object is a cookie you actually made from it. One class, many objects. Today you learn to design your own cutters and stamp out objects with them.

blocks.heading: Fields: what an object remembers
blocks.html:
Every object can remember things about itself. A dog remembers its name. A player remembers its score. Those memories are called fields (also called instance variables) — little boxes inside each object that hold its own values.

Below is a tiny class Dog with one field, name. Notice the rule: every runnable program needs a public class Main with main, and our helper class Dog sits in the same file without the word public.

blocks.caption: A class is a blueprint; new builds an object from it
blocks.explain: class Dog is the blueprint. new Dog() builds one real object and rex points to it. rex.name reaches inside that object to set and read its name field. Only Main is public; Dog is a plain helper class in the same file.

blocks.html: One public class per file. The file must be named after the public class, so it is Main.java. Helper classes like Dog live in the same file but are not marked public.

blocks.heading: Constructors and this 🛠️
blocks.html:
Setting rex.name by hand after building is clumsy. A constructor is a special set-up method that runs the moment you say new. It lets you hand over values right away, like filling in a form when you build the object.

Inside the constructor the word this means this exact object. So this.name = name reads as: “put the name I was given into MY own name box.” It tells apart the object's field from the value passed in.

blocks.caption: A constructor sets up the object; this points at itself
blocks.output:
Rex says: Woof!
Bella says: Woof!
blocks.explain: Dog(String name) is the constructor — it runs at new Dog("Rex"). this.name = name stores the given name in that object's own box. bark() is an instance method: each dog barks with its own name. Two separate objects, two separate names, one shared blueprint.

blocks.heading: Instance methods: things an object can DO
blocks.html: Fields are what an object knows; instance methods are what an object can do. A Player can score points. A Rectangle can work out its own area. Each method runs on one specific object and can use that object's own fields.

blocks.caption: Objects with their own data and behavior
blocks.explain: Player starts at score 0, then addScore(10) and addScore(5) grow its own score to 15. Rectangle(4, 3) stores width and height, and area() multiplies them to return 12. Three classes in one file, but still only one public class: Main.

blocks.html: new makes a fresh object every time. Two new Dog(...) calls give two separate dogs with separate memories. Changing one never touches the other.

blocks.question: What is the difference between a class and an object?
options: They are exactly the same thing
A class is the blueprint; an object is a real thing built from it with new
A class is faster than an object
An object is the blueprint; a class is built from it
blocks.explain: A class is the cookie cutter (blueprint). An object is a cookie stamped out of it using the new keyword. One class can make many objects.

blocks.question: Inside a constructor, what does this.name = name do?
options: Stores the value passed in into this object's own name field
Prints the name to the screen
blocks.explain: this means this exact object. this.name is the object's own field; name is the value handed to the constructor. So it saves the given value into this object's memory.

blocks.question: How many public classes can a single .java file have?
options: Exactly one, and the file is named after it
blocks.explain: Only one public class per file, and the file name must match it (Main.java). Helper classes in the same file are written without public.

blocks.title: Give the Cat a name and a meow
blocks.instructions: Finish the Cat class. Add a field name, a constructor that takes a name and stores it with this.name, and a meow() method that prints the cat's name followed by ' says: Meow!'. The Main code is already calling new Cat("Whiskers").
blocks.passMsg: Purr-fect! You built a class with a field, a constructor, and a method.
blocks.failMsg: Make sure your constructor uses this.name = name and meow() prints the name plus " says: Meow!".

blocks.title: Build two players from one blueprint
blocks.instructions: The Player class is finished. In main, create a SECOND player named "Luna", give her 20 points with addScore(20), then print her name and score just like Hero. One class, two separate objects!
blocks.solutionOutput:
Hero has 50 points
Luna has 20 points
blocks.passMsg: Two objects, two scores, one blueprint — that is the heart of OOP!
blocks.failMsg: Create a second Player with new Player("Luna"), call luna.addScore(20), then print luna.name and luna.score.

cards.back: Method with class name — runs on new

challenge.title: Design a BankAccount class
challenge.instructions: Build a BankAccount class with two fields: owner (String) and balance (int). Write a constructor that takes an owner name and starts the balance at 0. Add a deposit(int amount) method that adds to the balance, and a show() method that prints the owner and balance like 'Maya has 150 in the bank'. In main, create an account for "Maya", deposit 100, deposit 50, then call show().
challenge.hint: Start balance at 0 in the constructor. deposit adds to this.balance. After depositing 100 then 50, the balance is 150.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 You designed a whole class from scratch — fields, a constructor, and methods. Day 11 complete, you are thinking in objects now!
